sentences.text: I'm not-- not allowed to, you know, to read?
My name is Henry Weil, W-E-I-L.
I was born in Olkusz, O-L-K-U-S-Z , Poland.
I was born September the 10th, 1914.
Today I am 81.
My father's name-- Solomon.
And your mother?
HW: ?] Jadwiga.
Did you have any brothers or sisters?
Yes.
My brother's name is Wolf.
And my sisters-- Andzir and my-- and the other sister, Lola.
I was still young, and I was-- I was 18 years old.
We were-- becase-- before the war, I was working in a bank, Bank [?
Kupietzky, ?] for seven years.
Our hometown-- the hometown was [INAUDIBLE] in Olkusz.
We moved to Mirkow where my father used to live, and then a couple years later, we moved to Cracow.
In Cracow, we were till the end till-- till 1939.
Yeah.
Shabbat-- we were-- my father was a religious man.
In the same house where we used to live, you know, in Cracow, was a room in the same building, and my father has everything.
The service, has-- you know, he did, you know.
He, you know-- he did the Torah and everything, you know, all the time, you know.
And then only when the Germans went-- they came in to Cracow, you know, was-- was quiet, and only on the Shabbat.
And before the war, it was everyday, in the morning-- in the morning and the evening, also, every week.
Yes.
My mother, was-- she made [INAUDIBLE] and fish, you know?
My mother was a real, you know, Jewish woman, and a sweet one.
I went only to-- to public school.
Of course, I didn't have a chance to go higher because in the small town in Mirkow, you know, that's, you know, was too-- no one went, you know, to a high school, only public school.
And then we moved away to Cracow.
And in Cracow, my father has a store, a grocery store.
And I helped him, also, in this store.
Before?
It was very-- very-- the Jewish community was very nice, you know.
Almost everybody we know.
And-- and that's, of course, like life is supposed to be, like-- like a family, you know?
It was not too-- was maybe about 500 Jewish families.
But Cracow is a large city.
Yes.
I was a skate-- I-- I-- I liked to skate.
And I was skating on ice a couple years in Poland.
Then when we came to United States, I was skating.
And I teach, you know, my-- my and my daughter skating, also in the Rockefeller Center.
When I moved to Cracow, I-- I help my father in the store till I was 18 years old.
And then I-- I went to the Polish army.
And I was in the Polish army one and a half years from 1938 till 1939, just a few weeks before Hitler came to Poland, the war started.
The army was not bad, because in-- I-- I was in the army in Cracow, and we lived in Cracow.
The six months, you know, I couldn't go home for a couple hours with the street train.
But after, I could go everyday home for a couple hours from 7:00 to 11 o'clock.
As long, to be back, you know, for at night.
My brother moved to Cracow, and my-- and my little sister.
And my other sister, she got married, and she moved to Stanislawow.
That's in-- in Poland.
And then-- then-- then Hitler came in.
They said, you know, that it's impossible, you know, that's-- right away they will take men.
So my-- my brother-in-law, he had a chance.
He-- he ran away to Russia.
But-- and my sister was with a small baby, she was still in Stanislawow, because then we were in the ghetto, you know, when, you know-- My father tried to get somebody-- a Pollack-- a Polish man.
And he brought her home, you know, to be with us.
Did you experience any anti-Semitism?
Yes.
And-- when we had the store-- when he had the store, you know, I was at the time in a uniform-- in a Polish uniform.
They came-- one Pollack came at night, you know, and he broke the window from the-- from the-- from the basement.
And he went into the store, and he wanted to take out everything.
But-- and this-- this night, you know, I was home.
I was home, you know?
I went to him, you know, and I gave him a couple of times-- that he will remember forever.
You know, I wanted to kill him, because, for sure, in the cold, I will win.
Because he tried, you know, to-- the uniform, the uniform to take it, you know, to rip off from me, you know?
And that's not allowed.
And that's [INAUDIBLE].
The president, Marshal Pilsudski, was that time, and he was not an anti-Semite.
When Hitler came to power in 1933--
No, yeah--
As long as he was in Germany, it was OK.
Because as long, you know, in Poland, it was OK.
But 1930-- when he came in 1939, that was exactly September the 1st, 1939.
He came.
The first-- the first city was Cracow.
That-- that was very bad.
Took away all the stores, all the homes and everything, you know?
And he started right away from the beginning.
And he said to the Germans, you know, now you will have plenty homes from the Jews and plenty businesses and everything.
And they-- and he start-- he start sending-- you have more-- more people, more-- more-- more Germans who want to go with Hitler.
Because--
When he came to Cracow and was here-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oskar Schindler came up, because I have a cousin.
You know, I had a cousin.
His name was Abe Banker.
He has a factory, Emalia.
And he found out.
Oskar Schindler found out, you know, that we had the keys from the factory.
He came to us because my brother was the manager before the war in the factory.
So the keys were there.
And this was my cousin.
When he-- he came, he ate with us lunch-- Oskar Schindler.
And he told me stories [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] told-- that he is not a Nazi.
He is only in the party.
He want-- he want-- he doesn't care about the business, but he wants to show the world that he can help, you know, at least between 900 and 1,000 people-- that's what he did.
Yeah.
Yeah.
He told me, you know, that any time-- before he left, you know, first of all, before he left he gave my mother a kiss and my little sister a kiss.
And he-- I showed him to the door, and he said to me, Heinrich-- Henry is Heinrich-- any time, if you will be in Cracow-- because I told him that we are going to move to Wolbrom to a small town.
Anytime you will be in Cracow, in Plaszow, whatever is-- you know, in ghetto, try to see me.
Try to see me, because, you know, and over there, I will try the best for you.
And I follow-- that's what he told me, and he left.
About that he was very good, you know?
I remember, you know, that I was, you know, a policeman, you know.
But when I came back, you know, from-- that's a further burden.
When I came back-- when I came back, you know, to Plaszow, to the ghetto, and I was in-- in his-- in his place, you know, people-- the Jewish people were working very hard in the factory.
But he-- I got the best job, you know, in-- in the place over there.
And he gave me a lot of money, and a bunker, too, that's to give to the Pollacks.
And they will bring to you, everything that you wanted.
And he gave me a special place, you know, not a double bed, but he gave me a window that I will have more air and everything to be comfortable.
He was, to me, very nice.
He was to everybody nice.
After Cracow.
We heard rumors that it's-- it's no good to be in a big city, so we moved-- my parents-- and we all moved, you know, to Wolbrom.
In Wolbrom.
It's a small town about 50 miles from Cracow.
And over there, we came, you know, because my-- my father has a sister in Wolbrom.
When we came over there, we went straight to the ghetto.
And over there in the ghetto, we were two years, till 1942.
Nothing.
Just-- just a few things.
Just a few things, you know.
Just a few things that we-- we could get in the hands.
Small suitcases.
My father, my mother, and my two-- my-- my-- one-- the little sister.
And I was there.
Because my-- the other sister was in Stanislawow-- still in Stanislawow.
From Wolbrom, we-- my father tried to get, you know-- you know, the-- my-- my-- the other sister who was married, you know, to bring her so she would be able to be with us.
My brother was in Rzeszow, also in Poland, because he was married.
You know, and he was in-- in a-- in a ghetto in Rzeszow with his wife.
In Wolbrom, a couple of streets-- three, four streets, and that's-- that's it, you know?
And we had a small-- we had-- we had one room.
You can imagine, you know.
Before, we had five rooms, now we had-- and over there in Wolbrom.
But we were happy.
At least we are together.
Whatever will be, we'll be together.
Yeah.
Not by a wall, but, you know, but they were-- they were watching, you know-- the SS was watching.
No.
We could get it, you know.
Not clothing, you know, because we took some from home, you know, in the suitcase.
But my father's sister was living a long time in Wolbrom, so she helped us.
She helped us with food, you know.
She came every day, every second day.
[INAUDIBLE] next block.
And she brought us.
We didn't eat so much.
Not too good, you know?
We couldn't take every day a shower.
You know, a lot of things was no good, becasue-- but we were happy that we still alive.
No.
In ghetto?
No.
No, no, no schools.
Synagogues, you know, private.
Some-- sometimes, you know, the-- the-- the-- the service was in our-- in our room-- in our-- where we used to live, you know, it was a big room, you know.
How long it took-- a half an hour.
We were afraid.
We were watching, you know, from the window, you know, oh, SS men doesn't look in.
Nothing special.
Nothing special.
The selection, they made in 1942.
In 19-- made-- they liquidated the ghetto.
I don't exactly remember, but we were there close to two years.
In 1942, first-- first the liquidation.
The ghetto was-- there's a liquidation.
And-- the older people will not be able to go, so it was prepared, you know.
The [INAUDIBLE] prepared, you know, horses with-- with wagons.
And-- and there-- and there-- you know, they went over there.
At first, it was 450.
And between were my-- my father, also, because he was-- he was not able to go.
He was-- he had only one leg.
My father.
My mother and myself and my sister-- one sister-- you know, my two sisters, because the one from Stanislawow.
She was already in Wolbrom, too, with her baby.
We went, you know, to the railroad station.
You know, the policeman-- the Jewish policeman-- who was in ghetto, you know, came over.
Because they cleaned up the ghetto, so he came over.
You could go-- go.
So he said, you see it, Henry.
You know what?
Your father is dead.
I found, you know, his [INAUDIBLE], his [? select. ?] He-- but he came and he told me that.
And-- and then-- then in the same day, was liquidation, you know, the whole ghetto.
They took, you know, all the rest people.
My mother, my two sisters, myself, and the little baby, they took, you know, to the railroad station.
Over there was a-- we were over there a nice couple hours.
No.
And when we came over there, with us first, maybe about-- about 3,000 without food, without water.
When we came over there, wagons were already prepared.
And-- and so just the young men they took out, you know, from the group.
And they sent-- they send-- and they sent us away to [INAUDIBLE]-- to-- to Stalowa Wola.
Stalowa Wola-- that's in Poland, a steel factory.
And all the rest, you know, went into the black wagons, you know, and send to Chelmno.
To Chelmno, to death.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we-- I came to-- to Stalowa Wola.
No food and not water.
Because when we came over there, when we came to Stalowa Wola-- was-- not only from Wolbrom, you know, but it was from other-- other cities, also.
From small cities was also there.
Was all together, maybe between 9,000 and 10,000.
And the SS men, every night-- he walked like-- like he walked on a carpet-- killed about 10, 15 people.
When we get up in the morning, about 10, 15 people were dead.
That's what happened every night.
You mean in Stalowa Wola.
Stalowa Wola was a factory.
We-- we walked five miles-- five kilometers-- every day, each way.
When we came over there, we worked, you know, like everybody else.
You know.
It-- it-- it very-- labor-- labor work.
Labor work in the factory.
You know.
They-- they-- and they made steel.
You know, it was a very hard work, you know?
Sunday, one Sunday when we were not working, they gave us a soup.
And the soup was just plain water.
How I-- I survived so many weeks and months, I-- it's-- I can't understand.
Yeah.
Like-- like they-- like in-- in jail, you know, with the stripes and a number.
A number here, and a number on the back.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, you know, by the end, the American came and they found me whenever-- I'd been around.
I had run away.
We will [INAUDIBLE].
From the hometown?
Yes, my cousin.
My-- you know-- and he was-- he probably-- two days later, they shot him.
A few days later.
His name was Otto Weil.
Was a very close cousin.
We used to live in the same house, the same house, you know?
We were talking, you know, [INAUDIBLE] that everybody said the same thing.
Looks like we are next.
How-- how we-- we could see, you know, with our own eyes what a sight-- can-- can do it.
You know, how is he a miserable?
You know?
Once he said, you know, when he killed the people, he said, I would like to see how Jewish blood is running like water.
That's what he said.
I remember.
That's the SS man.
And I-- I don't want to say, but he-- he was a miserable.
He was some anti-Semite.
